rulings.Case Title: ROY MILLER et al VS. MARCUS DANIEL MERCHASIN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Compel Further Responses To Form Interoggs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, December 23, 2014, Line 2: Plaintiffs ROY MILLER, MARIO GALANDE, and MILLER - GALANDE ANTIQUES' Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion for relief from discovery order of Judge Goldsmith dated November 12, 2014 is denied. The plaintiff did not seek CCP 1008 relief directly from Judge Goldsmith. The basis for the requested relief pursuant CCP 473(b) which can be mandatory or discretionary. With no mention of CCP 473(b) in the Notice of Motion, mandatory relief in not considered. (See Lurie vs. Greenwald 132 Cal.Rptr.2d 680 (2003) The facts presented are do not support discretionary relief. The motion setting procedure in Department 302 (Discovery) is prior to a discovery hearing, when one or both parties decline to stipulate to the authority of the Judge Pro Tem, the Court Clerk and parties choose the continued hearing date. The date established on October 29, 2014 was November 12, 2014, which is unquestionably documented at the end of the hearing transcript and entered into the court minutes. Thereafter the report/recommendation to Judge Goldsmith from Judge Pro Tem Kane twice set forth the continued hearing date of November 12, 2014. Plaintiff's submission in support of the motion includes a declaration from Ken Coren, Esq. The Court discloses he has known Mr. Coren professionally and socially for many years. Hearing required regarding defendant's request for monetary sanctions. The parties to address whether a denial of CCP 473(b) discretionary relief supports the imposition of sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@knoxricksen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK BELANGER VS. WINE.COM et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication Of Issues
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 23, 2014, Line 5, DEFENDANT WINE.COM MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication Of Issues. The motion for summary adjudication is denied. Defendant failed to maintain its initial burden of production. The court denies summary adjudication as to the wrongful termination in violation of public policy cause of action. Plaintiff's fraud allegations support the wrongful termination claim. See Yau v. Santa Margarita Ford, Inc. (2014) 229 Cal.App.4th 144. The court denies summary adjudication as to the defamation cause of action. Whether Mr. Chow stated that he was underwhelmed with plaintiff's team and subsequently painted plaintiff as a liar when he denied the comment is a proveable assertion of fact. That was the issue raised in the moving papers. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether Mr. Chow made the false statement. There is also a triable issue of material fact whether he acted maliciously by denying the statement. The court does not entertain defendant's reliance on Coit Drapery Cleaners, Inc. v. Sequoia Ins. Co. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 1595, 1604, because that issue was raised for the first time in the reply brief. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CCP 437c(g) with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: ADELLE CHABLE VS. MIJITA RESTAURANT et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 23, 2014, Line 6, PLAINTIFF ADELLE CHABLE Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint. The motion to amend is granted in part. Plaintiff has leave to change the name of the defendant and delete causes of action 5-7. The remainder of the motion is denied because the proposed amendments are futile. Plaintiff cannot allege the age harassment and age discrimination causes of action because they are not related to or likely to be discovered during the investigation of the claims listed in her FEHA charge. See Okoli v. Lockheed Technical Operations Co., (1995) 36 Cal.App.4th 1607, 1615 ("a complaint alleging race discrimination is neither 'like or related to' nor likely to be discovered in a 'reasonable' investigation of a charge of sex discrimination."). The motion is denied as to adding Mr. Gomez as a defendant. Plaintiff was obligated to bring suit against him within one year of receiving the right to sue notice. See Yurick v. Sup. Crt. (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1116; Gov't 12965(b). Plaintiff has not filed suit against him within that time period. CCP 474 and the relation back doctrine do not apply to this case. Plaintiff fails to show that she was genuinely ignorant of his identity or the facts that created liability against him. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: LEONARD BAZA VS. THE HILLSTONE RESTAURANT GROUP, INC. A DELAWARE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant The Hillstone Restaurant Group, Inc.'S Demurrer To Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 23, 2014, Line 12, DEFENDANT THE HILLSTONE RESTAURANT GROUP, INC.'S Demurrer To Complaint. Demurrer sustained. While the receipt of services by Plaintiff from Defendant does not deprive Plaintiff of standing, the Court can and does find as a matter of law that Defendant's dress code is not arbitrary but rather is facially valid because it bears a reasonable relationship to commercial objectives appropriate to an enterprise serving the public. (Harris v. Capital Growth Investors XIV (1991) 52 Cal.3d 1142, 1165.) Plaintiff's intentional infliction of emotional distress claim fails because the Court can determine as a matter of law that Defendant's alleged conduct could not be determined by a trier of fact to be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Hughes v. Pair (2009) 46 Cal.4th 1035, 1050-51.) As Defendant prudently notes, "restaurants ask patrons to comply with dress codes, and even provide subpar service, every single day." As to Plaintiff's negligent infliction of emotional distress claim, while there is no duty to avoid negligently causing emotional distress, as a matter of law a defendant can be liable for this tort if the emotional distress arises out of a defendant's breach of some other legal duty and the emotional distress is proximately caused by that breach of duty. (Potter v. Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. (1993) 6 Cal.4th 965, 984.) Here, Plaintiff alleges that Defendant had a statutory duty pursuant to the Unruh Act, and that Defendant breached that duty. However, Plaintiff cannot establish as a matter of law that Defendant breached that duty by enforcing its dress code. As the defects in the Complaint cannot be cured by amendment, the Demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: LEONARD BAZA VS. THE HILLSTONE RESTAURANT GROUP, INC. A DELAWARE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Jonathan Geffard'S Demurrer To Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 23, 2014, Line 12, DEFENDANT JONATHAN GEFFARD'S Demurrer To Complaint. Demurrer sustained. While the receipt of services by Plaintiff from Defendant does not deprive Plaintiff of standing, the Court can and does find as a matter of law that Defendant's dress code is not arbitrary but rather is facially valid because it bears a reasonable relationship to commercial objectives appropriate to an enterprise serving the public. (Harris v. Capital Growth Investors XIV (1991) 52 Cal.3d 1142, 1165.) Plaintiff's intentional infliction of emotional distress claim fails because the Court can determine as a matter of law that Defendant's alleged conduct could not be determined by a trier of fact to be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Hughes v. Pair (2009) 46 Cal.4th 1035, 1050-51.) As Defendant prudently notes, "restaurants ask patrons to comply with dress codes, and even provide subpar service, every single day." As to Plaintiff's negligent infliction of emotional distress claim, while there is no duty to avoid negligently causing emotional distress, as a matter of law a defendant can be liable for this tort if the emotional distress arises out of a defendant's breach of some other legal duty and the emotional distress is proximately caused by that breach of duty. (Potter v. Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. (1993) 6 Cal.4th 965, 984.) Here, Plaintiff alleges that Defendant had a statutory duty pursuant to the Unruh Act, and that Defendant breached that duty. However, Plaintiff cannot establish as a matter of law that Defendant breached that duty by enforcing its dress code. As the defects in the Complaint cannot be cured by amendment, the Demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)
